title: Day 10 Mega Vocabulary — Revision of Days 1-9 (500+ Words)

words.simpleMeaning: to put food in mouth and swallow

sentences.daily: I eat rice and dal every day.
sentences.office: We eat lunch at 1 PM.
sentences.interview: I eat healthy food to stay focused.
sentences.business: Let's eat together after the meeting.
sentences.formal: He eats in the cafeteria regularly.
sentences.informal: What do you eat for breakfast?

words.usageNote: 'Eat' is used for all meals. 'Have' is also commonly used: I have lunch.

words.simpleMeaning: to swallow liquid

sentences.daily: I drink water every morning.
sentences.office: She drinks coffee before work.
sentences.interview: I drink green tea to stay alert.
sentences.business: Let's drink to our success.
sentences.formal: He drinks water during meetings.
sentences.informal: Do you drink juice or milk?

words.usageNote: 'Drink' is for liquids. 'Drunk' is past participle, 'drank' is simple past.

words.simpleMeaning: to move from one place to another

sentences.daily: I go to school every day.
sentences.office: She goes to office by metro.
sentences.interview: I go to interviews well prepared.
sentences.business: We go to client meetings every week.
sentences.formal: He goes abroad for conferences.

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: go → went → gone. Very commonly used.

words.simpleMeaning: to move toward a place or person

sentences.daily: Please come here.
sentences.office: She comes to office on time.
sentences.interview: I come prepared for every interview.
sentences.business: New clients come every month.
sentences.formal: He comes from a strong academic background.

words.usageNote: Note: past participle of 'come' is 'come' (same as base form).

words.simpleMeaning: to take part in a game or activity for fun

sentences.daily: I play cricket on weekends.
sentences.office: We play team-building games sometimes.
sentences.interview: I play chess to sharpen my mind.
sentences.business: We play a key role in this project.
sentences.formal: He plays an important part in the team.

words.usageNote: 'Play' is used for games and sports. Also used figuratively: play a role.

words.simpleMeaning: to learn about a subject

sentences.daily: I study English every night.
sentences.office: We study market trends regularly.
sentences.interview: I studied hard for this position.
sentences.business: Let's study the data before deciding.
sentences.formal: She studies consumer behaviour.
sentences.informal: How do you study for exams?

words.usageNote: 'Studies' for he/she/it. 'Study' changes to 'studies' in third person.

words.simpleMeaning: to do a job or task

sentences.daily: I work from 9 to 6.
sentences.office: She works in the HR department.
sentences.interview: I work well in a team environment.
sentences.business: We work with multiple clients.
sentences.formal: He works diligently on all projects.

words.usageNote: Both noun and verb. 'Work' as noun: 'I have a lot of work.'

words.simpleMeaning: to give knowledge or instruction to someone

sentences.daily: My mother teaches me good habits.
sentences.office: She teaches new employees the process.
sentences.interview: I can teach and mentor junior staff.
sentences.business: We teach clients how to use our product.
sentences.formal: He teaches communication skills to executives.

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: teach → taught → taught.

words.simpleMeaning: to gain knowledge or a skill

sentences.daily: I learn something new every day.
sentences.office: We learn from our mistakes.
sentences.interview: I learn quickly in new environments.
sentences.business: Let's learn from this case study.
sentences.formal: She learns by doing, not just reading.

words.usageNote: Can also use 'learnt' (British English) as past form.

words.simpleMeaning: to rest with eyes closed and be unconscious

sentences.daily: I sleep for 7 hours every night.
sentences.office: He sleeps late and comes late to work.
sentences.interview: I sleep well to stay focused.
sentences.business: Good leaders sleep well despite stress.
sentences.formal: She sleeps early to maintain good health.
sentences.informal: How many hours do you sleep?

words.usageNote: Irregular: sleep → slept → slept.

words.simpleMeaning: to stop sleeping and become conscious

sentences.daily: I wake up at 6 AM every day.
sentences.office: She wakes up early for exercise.
sentences.interview: I wake up early and prepare well.
sentences.business: Leaders wake up to new challenges daily.
sentences.formal: He wakes at dawn to review reports.
sentences.informal: What time do you wake up?

words.usageNote: 'Wake up' (phrasal verb) is more common than just 'wake'.

words.simpleMeaning: to move quickly on foot

sentences.daily: I run 5 km every morning.
sentences.office: She runs the entire operations team.
sentences.interview: I run projects from planning to delivery.
sentences.business: We run this company efficiently.
sentences.formal: He runs three departments simultaneously.

words.usageNote: Irregular: run → ran → run. Also means 'to manage': run a company.

words.simpleMeaning: to move on foot at a normal pace

sentences.daily: I walk to the market every evening.
sentences.office: She walks around the office for energy.
sentences.interview: I walk in prepared and confident.
sentences.business: Let's walk through the plan together.
sentences.formal: He walks to maintain his health.
sentences.informal: Do you walk or take the bus?

words.usageNote: 'Walk through' means to explain step by step: Let's walk through this.

words.simpleMeaning: to speak; to have a conversation

sentences.daily: I talk to my family every evening.
sentences.office: She talks to clients professionally.
sentences.interview: I can talk about my experience in detail.
sentences.business: Let's talk business now.
sentences.formal: He talks with confidence in meetings.
sentences.informal: Can we talk for a minute?

words.usageNote: 'Talk' is informal, 'speak' is more formal. 'Talk to' vs 'talk with' — both are correct.

words.simpleMeaning: to pay attention to sounds or speech

sentences.daily: I listen to English podcasts daily.
sentences.office: She listens carefully in meetings.
sentences.interview: I listen actively to understand questions.
sentences.business: Good leaders listen before they speak.
sentences.formal: He listens to feedback and improves.

words.usageNote: 'Listen' requires attention; 'hear' is passive. Always 'listen to' (not 'listen').

words.simpleMeaning: to look at and understand written words

sentences.daily: I read the newspaper every morning.
sentences.office: She reads all reports before meetings.
sentences.interview: I read industry news to stay updated.
sentences.business: Please read the contract carefully.
sentences.formal: He reads extensively to build knowledge.
sentences.informal: What do you like to read?

words.usageNote: Interesting: 'read' (present) and 'read' (past) are spelled same but pronounced differently: /riːd/ vs /red/.

words.simpleMeaning: to form words on a surface with a pen or keyboard

sentences.daily: I write in my diary every night.
sentences.office: She writes detailed project reports.
sentences.interview: I write well and communicate clearly.
sentences.business: Please write a summary of today's meeting.
sentences.formal: He writes professional emails to clients.
sentences.informal: Can you write your number here?

words.usageNote: Irregular: write → wrote → written.

words.simpleMeaning: to prepare food using heat

sentences.daily: My mother cooks delicious food.
sentences.office: She cooks for the office party.
sentences.interview: Cooking is one of my hobbies.
sentences.business: We cook up new strategies every quarter.
sentences.formal: He cooks meals for charity events.

words.usageNote: 'Cook up' idiomatically means 'to plan/invent': They cooked up a plan.

words.simpleMeaning: to remove dirt or make something tidy

sentences.daily: I clean my room every Sunday.
sentences.office: She cleans her desk before starting work.
sentences.interview: I keep my workspace clean and organized.
sentences.business: Let's clean up the data before analysis.
sentences.formal: He cleans his schedule to focus on key tasks.
sentences.informal: Did you clean your room yet?

words.usageNote: Also an adjective: 'a clean workspace'. 'Clean up' means to organize.

words.simpleMeaning: to make it easier for someone to do something

sentences.daily: I help my mother in the kitchen.
sentences.office: She helps new colleagues settle in.
sentences.interview: I enjoy helping others solve problems.
sentences.business: Our product helps clients save time.
sentences.formal: He helps the team achieve its goals.

words.usageNote: 'Help' can be followed by base verb: help me do (not 'to do' — both are acceptable).

words.simpleMeaning: to get something by paying money

sentences.daily: I buy vegetables from the market.
sentences.office: She buys office supplies monthly.
sentences.interview: I bought this course to improve my skills.
sentences.business: We buy raw materials from local vendors.
sentences.formal: He buys only premium quality products.

words.usageNote: Irregular: buy → bought → bought.

words.simpleMeaning: to give something in exchange for money

sentences.daily: My uncle sells vegetables.
sentences.office: She sells insurance products.
sentences.interview: I have experience in selling B2B solutions.
sentences.business: We sell across 20 states in India.
sentences.formal: He sells premium services to corporate clients.

words.usageNote: Irregular: sell → sold → sold. 'Sales' is the noun form.

words.simpleMeaning: to make musical sounds with your voice

sentences.daily: I sing in the shower every morning.
sentences.office: She sings at the company annual event.
sentences.interview: Singing is my hobby and stress reliever.
sentences.business: We sing the company song at every retreat.
sentences.formal: He sings in the corporate choir.
sentences.informal: Can you sing a song for us?

words.usageNote: Irregular: sing → sang → sung.

words.simpleMeaning: to move your body rhythmically to music

sentences.daily: I dance at every family function.
sentences.office: She dances at the company Diwali party.
sentences.interview: Dancing teaches me discipline and coordination.
sentences.business: Good negotiators dance around difficult topics gracefully.
sentences.formal: He dances at cultural events.
sentences.informal: Do you know how to dance?

words.usageNote: 'Dance around' idiomatically means to avoid a topic.

words.simpleMeaning: to move through water using your body

sentences.daily: I swim every morning for fitness.
sentences.office: She swims to manage work stress.
sentences.interview: Swimming taught me endurance and discipline.
sentences.business: Good businesses swim with market trends.
sentences.formal: He swims competitively at national level.

words.usageNote: Irregular: swim → swam → swum.

words.simpleMeaning: to operate and control a vehicle

sentences.daily: I drive to office every day.
sentences.office: She drives 30 km for work.
sentences.interview: I drive myself to achieve ambitious goals.
sentences.business: What drives your business growth?
sentences.formal: He drives the strategic vision of the company.

words.usageNote: Irregular: drive → drove → driven. Figuratively means 'to motivate'.

words.simpleMeaning: to cease moving or doing something

sentences.daily: Stop talking and listen.
sentences.office: She stops work at exactly 6 PM.
sentences.interview: I stop and think before answering.
sentences.business: We stopped the project due to budget issues.
sentences.formal: He stops to reflect before making decisions.

words.usageNote: 'Stop + ing' = cease doing. 'Stop + to + infinitive' = pause in order to do.

words.simpleMeaning: to begin something

sentences.daily: I start my day at 6 AM.
sentences.office: She starts every meeting with a summary.
sentences.interview: I started this job fresh out of college.
sentences.business: We start new projects every quarter.
sentences.formal: He starts each presentation with data.

words.usageNote: 'Start' and 'begin' are synonyms. 'Start up' means to launch a new business.

words.simpleMeaning: to complete or bring to an end

sentences.daily: I finish my homework before dinner.
sentences.office: She finishes all reports on time.
sentences.interview: I always finish tasks before deadlines.
sentences.business: We need to finish this project by Friday.
sentences.formal: He finishes every task with full attention.

words.usageNote: 'Finish + ing': I finished working. Always followed by gerund (ing form).

words.simpleMeaning: to move so that passage is possible; not closed

sentences.daily: Open the window please.
sentences.office: She opens the office at 9 AM.
sentences.interview: I am open to learning new skills.
sentences.business: We opened a new branch last month.
sentences.formal: He opens each session with an agenda.

words.usageNote: Also an adjective: 'an open mind'. 'Open to' means willing to consider.

words.simpleMeaning: to shut something; to bring to an end

sentences.daily: Close the door before sleeping.
sentences.office: She closes her laptop after work.
sentences.interview: I close deals effectively.
sentences.business: We need to close this deal this week.
sentences.formal: He closes every presentation with a summary.

words.usageNote: Close (verb) = /kloʊz/; Close (adjective, meaning near) = /kloʊs/. Different pronunciation!

words.simpleMeaning: to pick up or carry something

sentences.daily: I take the bus to work.
sentences.office: She takes notes in every meeting.
sentences.interview: I take ownership of my responsibilities.
sentences.business: Take this opportunity seriously.
sentences.formal: He takes a strategic approach to problems.

words.usageNote: Irregular: take → took → taken. Many phrasal verbs: take off, take over, take up.

words.simpleMeaning: to hand over or provide something to someone

sentences.daily: I give my best effort every day.
sentences.office: She gives constructive feedback.
sentences.interview: I can give you a strong reference.
sentences.business: We give discounts to loyal clients.
sentences.formal: He gives presentations to senior management.
sentences.informal: Can you give me a pen?

words.usageNote: Irregular: give → gave → given. 'Give up' means to stop trying.

words.simpleMeaning: to cause something to go to another place

sentences.daily: I send messages to my friends daily.
sentences.office: She sends emails to all clients.
sentences.interview: I will send you my resume.
sentences.business: Please send the invoice today.
sentences.formal: He sends detailed reports to management.

words.usageNote: Irregular: send → sent → sent.

words.simpleMeaning: to use your mind to consider something

sentences.daily: I think before I speak.
sentences.office: She thinks creatively to solve problems.
sentences.interview: I think this role is perfect for me.
sentences.business: Think long-term, not just short-term.
sentences.formal: He thinks strategically about market trends.

words.usageNote: Irregular: think → thought → thought. 'Think about' = consider; 'Think of' = imagine.

words.simpleMeaning: to have information about something

sentences.daily: I know how to speak English.
sentences.office: She knows all the company policies.
sentences.interview: I know this industry very well.
sentences.business: Know your competition inside out.
sentences.formal: He knows the technical details thoroughly.

words.usageNote: Irregular: know → knew → known. Stative verb — don't use in continuous form.

words.simpleMeaning: to experience a physical or emotional sensation

sentences.daily: I feel happy today.
sentences.office: She feels comfortable in her role.
sentences.interview: I feel confident about this position.
sentences.business: Customers need to feel valued.
sentences.formal: He feels strongly about quality.

words.usageNote: Irregular: feel → felt → felt. 'Feel like' = have a desire for.

words.simpleMeaning: to perceive with your eyes

sentences.daily: I see a bird outside my window.
sentences.office: She sees the big picture clearly.
sentences.interview: I see myself growing in this company.
sentences.business: Let's see how this performs.
sentences.formal: He sees opportunities where others see problems.

words.usageNote: Irregular: see → saw → seen. Stative verb — don't use in continuous for vision.

words.simpleMeaning: to receive sound through your ears

sentences.daily: I hear birds every morning.
sentences.office: She heard the good news about the project.
sentences.interview: I hear good things about your company.
sentences.business: Have you heard about our new product?
sentences.formal: He hears concerns from all team members.

words.usageNote: Irregular: hear → heard → heard. 'Hear' = passive (sound comes to you). 'Listen' = active (you pay attention).

words.simpleMeaning: to look at something carefully

sentences.daily: I watch English videos every day.
sentences.office: She watches her team's progress closely.
sentences.interview: I watch how successful people work.
sentences.business: Watch the market trends carefully.
sentences.formal: He watches industry developments closely.

words.usageNote: 'Watch' implies focused attention. 'Watch out!' = be careful.

words.simpleMeaning: to stay in one place until something happens

sentences.daily: I wait for the bus every morning.
sentences.office: She waits for approval before proceeding.
sentences.interview: I can wait for the right opportunity.
sentences.business: Don't wait — the market won't wait for you.
sentences.formal: He waits for all stakeholders before deciding.

words.usageNote: 'Wait for' = wait for a person/thing. 'Wait on' = serve someone (in a restaurant).

words.simpleMeaning: to request information or help from someone

sentences.daily: I ask my teacher when I have doubts.
sentences.office: She asks for feedback regularly.
sentences.interview: I ask thoughtful questions in interviews.
sentences.business: Always ask for the business.
sentences.formal: He asks relevant questions in every meeting.

words.usageNote: 'Ask for' = request something. 'Ask about' = inquire about a topic.

words.simpleMeaning: to respond to a question or request

sentences.daily: I answer my parents' questions honestly.
sentences.office: She answers all client emails within an hour.
sentences.interview: I answer every question with examples.
sentences.business: Answer the customer's needs first.
sentences.formal: He answers queries with detailed information.

words.usageNote: 'Answer the phone' = pick up the phone and speak.

words.simpleMeaning: to communicate information to someone

sentences.daily: Please tell me your name.
sentences.office: She tells her team the goals clearly.
sentences.interview: Let me tell you about my achievements.
sentences.business: Tell the client exactly what to expect.
sentences.formal: He tells the board the quarterly results.

words.usageNote: Irregular: tell → told → told. Always 'tell someone': tell me, tell him (not 'tell to me').

words.simpleMeaning: to allow someone to see or know something

sentences.daily: Show me your work.
sentences.office: She shows initiative in every project.
sentences.interview: I will show you my portfolio.
sentences.business: Numbers show our growth clearly.
sentences.formal: He shows excellent leadership qualities.

words.usageNote: 'Show off' = to display something boastfully.

words.simpleMeaning: to discover or locate something

sentences.daily: I find time to study every day.
sentences.office: She finds solutions quickly.
sentences.interview: I find challenges motivating.
sentences.business: We need to find new revenue streams.
sentences.formal: He finds patterns in complex data.

words.usageNote: Irregular: find → found → found. 'Find out' = discover information.

words.simpleMeaning: to be unable to find something; to not win

sentences.daily: Don't lose your keys.
sentences.office: We don't want to lose this client.
sentences.interview: I never lose focus under pressure.
sentences.business: A bad service can make you lose customers.
sentences.formal: He does not lose sight of his objectives.

words.usageNote: Irregular: lose → lost → lost. 'Lose' vs 'loose' — common mistake. 'Loose' = not tight.

words.simpleMeaning: to employ something for a purpose

sentences.daily: I use my phone for learning English.
sentences.office: She uses Excel for all data analysis.
sentences.interview: I use data to make decisions.
sentences.business: Use technology to your advantage.
sentences.formal: He uses a structured approach for planning.

words.usageNote: 'Used to' (past habit): I used to live here. Different from 'use to' which is incorrect.

words.simpleMeaning: to create or produce something

sentences.daily: I make tea every morning.
sentences.office: She makes a to-do list every day.
sentences.interview: I make sure all deadlines are met.
sentences.business: Let's make this deal happen.
sentences.formal: He makes informed decisions.
sentences.informal: Can you make some time for me?

words.usageNote: Irregular: make → made → made. Many collocations: make a decision, make a call, make an effort.

words.simpleMeaning: to make an effort to do something

sentences.daily: I try to read English every day.
sentences.office: She tries a new approach each time.
sentences.interview: I always try my best.
sentences.business: Let's try a different strategy.
sentences.formal: He tries to resolve conflicts peacefully.

words.usageNote: 'Try to do' = attempt. 'Try doing' = experiment. Both are correct but mean slightly different things.

words.simpleMeaning: to say words; to communicate verbally

sentences.daily: I speak Hindi and English.
sentences.office: She speaks to clients professionally.
sentences.interview: I can speak fluently in English.
sentences.business: Speak clearly in business meetings.
sentences.formal: He speaks with great confidence.
sentences.informal: Just speak up, don't be shy.

words.usageNote: 'Speak' is more formal than 'talk'. Irregular: speak → spoke → spoken.

words.simpleMeaning: to know the meaning of something

sentences.daily: I understand the grammar rule now.
sentences.office: She understands client needs well.
sentences.interview: I understand the challenges of this role.
sentences.business: Understanding your market is key.
sentences.formal: He understands complex technical concepts.

words.usageNote: Irregular: understand → understood → understood. Stative verb — avoid continuous form.

words.simpleMeaning: to bring something back to mind

sentences.daily: I remember all the vocabulary.
sentences.office: She remembers every client's name.
sentences.interview: I remember my goals every morning.
sentences.business: Always remember: customer is king.
sentences.formal: He remembers every detail of the deal.

words.usageNote: 'Remember to do' = don't forget. 'Remember doing' = recall a past action.

words.simpleMeaning: to fail to remember something

sentences.daily: Don't forget to revise daily.
sentences.office: She never forgets a deadline.
sentences.interview: I never forget lessons from my mistakes.
sentences.business: Don't forget to follow up with clients.
sentences.formal: He forgets nothing that matters.

words.usageNote: Irregular: forget → forgot → forgotten. 'Forget to do' = missed doing. 'Forget doing' = forget a memory.

words.simpleMeaning: to do something repeatedly to improve

sentences.daily: I practice English for one hour daily.
sentences.office: She practices her presentations before delivery.
sentences.interview: I practice mock interviews regularly.
sentences.business: Practice makes perfect in sales too.
sentences.formal: He practices what he preaches.

words.usageNote: In British English, the noun is 'practice' and verb is 'practise'. In American English, both are 'practice'.

words.simpleMeaning: to present someone or something for the first time

sentences.daily: Let me introduce myself.
sentences.office: She introduces new team members every Monday.
sentences.interview: Please introduce yourself briefly.
sentences.business: Allow me to introduce our new product.
sentences.formal: He introduced himself to the panel confidently.

words.usageNote: 'Introduce yourself' = give your own introduction. Day 2 key phrase.

words.simpleMeaning: to say hello or welcome someone

sentences.daily: I greet my neighbors every morning.
sentences.office: She greets every client with a smile.
sentences.interview: I greeted the panel confidently.
sentences.business: Always greet clients warmly.
sentences.formal: He greets all visitors professionally.

words.usageNote: Formal greetings: Good morning/afternoon/evening. Informal: Hi, Hello.

words.simpleMeaning: to greet someone gladly when they arrive

sentences.daily: Welcome to our home!
sentences.office: She welcomes new joiners warmly.
sentences.interview: Thank you for welcoming me today.
sentences.business: We welcome this opportunity to serve you.
sentences.formal: He welcomed the delegation at the airport.

words.usageNote: 'You are welcome' = response to 'thank you'. 'Welcome' as noun: a warm welcome.

words.simpleMeaning: to be a member of or connected to something

sentences.daily: I belong to a joint family.
sentences.office: She belongs to the finance department.
sentences.interview: I belong to a strong work culture background.
sentences.business: This brand belongs in the premium segment.
sentences.formal: He belongs to a reputable institution.

words.usageNote: 'Belong to' = be a member of. Do not use in continuous: say 'I belong' not 'I am belonging'.

words.simpleMeaning: to focus on or be expert in a particular field

sentences.daily: I specialize in English grammar.
sentences.office: She specializes in digital marketing.
sentences.interview: I specialize in data analytics.
sentences.business: Our company specializes in IT solutions.
sentences.formal: He specializes in corporate law.

words.usageNote: 'Specialize in' — always use 'in' after specialize.

words.simpleMeaning: knowledge or skill gained by doing something

sentences.daily: I have 5 years of experience.
sentences.office: She brings valuable experience to the team.
sentences.interview: My experience includes project management.
sentences.business: Experience builds trust with clients.
sentences.formal: He has extensive experience in finance.
sentences.informal: How much experience do you have?

words.usageNote: Both noun and verb. 'Experience' as verb = to go through: I experienced failure and learned.

words.simpleMeaning: to have the right skills or qualifications for something

sentences.daily: I qualify for this scholarship.
sentences.office: She qualifies for a promotion.
sentences.interview: I am qualified for this position.
sentences.business: Our product qualifies as premium-grade.
sentences.formal: He qualifies candidates through a rigorous process.

words.usageNote: 'Qualified' as adjective: a qualified engineer. 'Qualify for' = meet the criteria for something.

words.simpleMeaning: to complete a course of study and receive a degree

sentences.daily: I graduated from Delhi University.
sentences.office: She graduated with top honours.
sentences.interview: I graduated in Computer Science in 2020.
sentences.business: Our graduate programme attracts top talent.
sentences.formal: He graduated with distinction.

words.usageNote: 'Graduate from' a university (not 'graduate in'). 'Graduate in' a subject is informal usage.

words.simpleMeaning: to successfully complete a goal or task

sentences.daily: I achieve my daily study goals.
sentences.office: She achieves all her quarterly targets.
sentences.interview: I achieved a 30% increase in sales last year.
sentences.business: Our team achieved record revenue.
sentences.formal: He achieved his objectives within budget.
sentences.informal: Did you achieve your goal today?

words.usageNote: 'Achieve' is commonly used in interviews and resumes. Followed by a noun: achieve success, achieve targets.

words.simpleMeaning: to speak or act on behalf of someone

sentences.daily: I represent my family at functions.
sentences.office: She represents the marketing team in meetings.
sentences.interview: I represented my college in national debates.
sentences.business: I represent our company in client negotiations.
sentences.formal: He represents the interests of all shareholders.

words.usageNote: Commonly used in professional introductions: 'I represent XYZ company.'

words.simpleMeaning: a job that requires special training or education

sentences.daily: Teaching is a noble profession.
sentences.office: What is your profession?
sentences.interview: My profession is software engineering.
sentences.business: This profession demands continuous learning.
sentences.formal: He is a professional in his field.

words.usageNote: Noun only. 'Professional' is the adjective/noun form: a true professional.

words.simpleMeaning: a person's education, experience, and family origins

sentences.daily: My background is from a small town.
sentences.office: Her background in finance is impressive.
sentences.interview: My background includes 5 years in IT.
sentences.business: Our team has a diverse professional background.
sentences.formal: He comes from a strong academic background.

words.usageNote: Common in interviews: 'Tell me about your background.' Usually means education + work experience.

words.simpleMeaning: a good quality or ability you have

sentences.daily: My strength is my dedication.
sentences.office: Team strength lies in collaboration.
sentences.interview: My biggest strength is problem solving.
sentences.business: Product strength drives sales.
sentences.formal: He demonstrates great strength under pressure.

words.usageNote: Very common interview question: 'What are your strengths?' Answer with examples.

words.simpleMeaning: an area where you are not as strong or capable

sentences.daily: My weakness is procrastination.
sentences.office: Every team has weaknesses to work on.
sentences.interview: My weakness is I work too hard — but I am improving.
sentences.business: Know your competitors' weaknesses.
sentences.formal: He acknowledges his weaknesses honestly.

words.usageNote: 'Weakness' interview tip: mention a real weakness but say how you are improving it.

words.simpleMeaning: a strong desire to achieve something in life

sentences.daily: My ambition is to speak fluent English.
sentences.office: Her ambition drives the whole team.
sentences.interview: My ambition is to become a senior manager.
sentences.business: Ambition and execution drive success.
sentences.formal: He shows great ambition in every role.

words.usageNote: 'Ambitious' is the adjective. 'I am ambitious' = I want to achieve big things.

words.simpleMeaning: a strong feeling of enthusiasm for something

sentences.daily: My passion is learning English.
sentences.office: She brings passion to every project.
sentences.interview: I have a passion for customer service.
sentences.business: Passion fuels business success.
sentences.formal: He demonstrates remarkable passion for his work.

words.usageNote: 'Passionate about' = feeling strong enthusiasm for: I am passionate about teaching.

words.simpleMeaning: great skill or knowledge in a particular area

sentences.daily: I am building expertise in English.
sentences.office: Her expertise in marketing is invaluable.
sentences.interview: My expertise lies in software development.
sentences.business: Our firm's expertise sets us apart.
sentences.formal: He has deep expertise in financial modelling.

words.usageNote: 'Area of expertise' is a very common professional phrase. Use in introductions.

words.simpleMeaning: the ability or power to do something

sentences.daily: I believe in my capability.
sentences.office: She has the capability to lead the team.
sentences.interview: I have the capability to handle pressure.
sentences.business: Our team's capability is our biggest asset.
sentences.formal: He has demonstrated remarkable capability.
sentences.informal: Do you have the capability for this?

words.usageNote: Noun form. 'Capable' is adjective: I am capable of this task.

words.simpleMeaning: commitment to a task or purpose

sentences.daily: My dedication helps me complete the challenge.
sentences.office: She shows total dedication to the company.
sentences.interview: My dedication to quality is my key strength.
sentences.business: Dedication to customers builds loyalty.
sentences.formal: He demonstrates exceptional dedication.

words.usageNote: 'Dedicated' as adjective: a dedicated employee. 'Dedication to' = commitment toward something.

words.simpleMeaning: to share information or express ideas

sentences.daily: I communicate in English every day.
sentences.office: She communicates clearly with her team.
sentences.interview: I communicate effectively under pressure.
sentences.business: Good businesses communicate transparently.
sentences.formal: He communicates complex ideas simply.

words.usageNote: Key skill for interviews. 'Communication skills' = ability to express yourself clearly.

words.simpleMeaning: to build professional relationships

sentences.daily: I network at every professional event.
sentences.office: She networks with industry leaders.
sentences.interview: I have a wide professional network.
sentences.business: Networking opens new business doors.
sentences.formal: He networks to discover partnership opportunities.

words.usageNote: Both noun and verb. 'Networking' as gerund: Networking is essential for career growth.

words.simpleMeaning: to work together with others toward a shared goal

sentences.daily: I collaborate with classmates on projects.
sentences.office: She collaborates across departments smoothly.
sentences.interview: I enjoy collaborating with diverse teams.
sentences.business: We collaborate with global partners.
sentences.formal: He collaborates with experts in the field.

words.usageNote: 'Collaborate with' or 'collaborate on': collaborate with the team on a project.

words.simpleMeaning: to be in charge of and control something

sentences.daily: I manage my time well.
sentences.office: She manages a team of 15 people.
sentences.interview: I managed a cross-functional project.
sentences.business: Good managers manage results, not people.
sentences.formal: He manages the entire supply chain.

words.usageNote: 'Manage to do' = succeed in doing: I managed to finish on time.

words.simpleMeaning: to give details about someone or something

sentences.daily: Describe your daily routine in English.
sentences.office: She describes the project scope clearly.
sentences.interview: Describe a challenge you overcame.
sentences.business: Describe your company's USP.
sentences.formal: He describes the process step by step.

words.usageNote: Common interview instruction: 'Describe yourself.' = Tell us about yourself.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something clear by giving details

sentences.daily: I explain grammar rules simply.
sentences.office: She explains the process to new joiners.
sentences.interview: Please explain your gap year.
sentences.business: Explain the value proposition clearly.
sentences.formal: He explains complex data in simple terms.

words.usageNote: 'Explain to someone': explain to me (not 'explain me'). Preposition is essential.

words.simpleMeaning: a hope or ambition to achieve something

sentences.daily: My aspiration is to be fluent in English.
sentences.office: Her aspirations drive her career forward.
sentences.interview: My aspiration is to lead a global team.
sentences.business: Aspirations backed by action create success.
sentences.formal: He shared his long-term aspirations clearly.

words.usageNote: 'Aspiration' is the noun; 'aspire' is the verb: I aspire to become a leader.

words.simpleMeaning: to give an order; to be in authority

sentences.daily: Teachers command students to be silent.
sentences.office: She commands great respect in the team.
sentences.interview: I can command a team effectively.
sentences.business: A strong leader commands through trust.
sentences.formal: He commands the respect of every colleague.

words.usageNote: 'Command respect' = earn respect through authority. Used in Day 3 — Imperative context.

words.simpleMeaning: to give someone directions or orders

sentences.daily: My teacher instructs me daily.
sentences.office: She instructs the team before every task.
sentences.interview: I was instructed to prepare a detailed plan.
sentences.business: Please instruct the team on the new process.
sentences.formal: He instructs new employees on safety protocols.
sentences.informal: Just instruct me what to do.

words.usageNote: More formal than 'tell'. 'Instruct someone to do something' = give instructions.

words.simpleMeaning: to show someone the way or advise them

sentences.daily: Guide me through this exercise.
sentences.office: She guides junior employees in their work.
sentences.interview: My mentor guided me to this career.
sentences.business: A vision guides business decisions.
sentences.formal: He guides the team with clarity.

words.usageNote: Imperative form: Guide me! Also used as noun: a guide book.

words.simpleMeaning: to go after someone or do as directed

sentences.daily: Follow the grammar rules carefully.
sentences.office: She follows the company policy strictly.
sentences.interview: I always follow up after interviews.
sentences.business: Follow the process for consistency.
sentences.formal: He follows all legal guidelines.

words.usageNote: 'Follow up' = take action after something. 'Follow through' = complete what you started.

words.simpleMeaning: to do what you are told or follow rules

sentences.daily: Children should obey their parents.
sentences.office: Everyone must obey company rules.
sentences.interview: I obey professional ethics at all times.
sentences.business: Businesses must obey laws and regulations.
sentences.formal: He always obeys the chain of command.

words.usageNote: 'Obey' is slightly formal. Everyday alternative: 'follow the rules'.

words.simpleMeaning: to tell someone about a possible danger

sentences.daily: I warned you about this mistake.
sentences.office: She warns the team about deadlines.
sentences.interview: I always warn about risks honestly.
sentences.business: Warn clients of potential delays.
sentences.formal: He warned the board of financial risks.

words.usageNote: 'Warn someone about something' or 'warn someone not to do something'.

words.simpleMeaning: to give someone a recommendation

sentences.daily: I advise you to practice daily.
sentences.office: She advises management on HR policies.
sentences.interview: I advised my team to improve communication.
sentences.business: Our firm advises clients on investments.
sentences.formal: He advises the board on strategic matters.
sentences.informal: I'd advise you not to do that.

words.usageNote: 'Advise' = give advice. Noun: advice. 'Advise someone to do': I advise you to practice.

words.simpleMeaning: to cause someone to remember something

sentences.daily: Remind me to study tonight.
sentences.office: She reminds the team about deadlines.
sentences.interview: This question reminds me of a real situation.
sentences.business: Please remind clients about the event.
sentences.formal: He reminds everyone of the meeting agenda.

words.usageNote: 'Remind someone to do something' or 'remind someone about something'.

words.simpleMeaning: to put forward an idea for consideration

sentences.daily: I suggest you practice every day.
sentences.office: She suggests a new method for tracking.
sentences.interview: I suggested a cost-saving idea last year.
sentences.business: What do you suggest we do next?
sentences.formal: He suggests an alternative approach.

words.usageNote: 'Suggest + ing' or 'suggest + that clause': I suggest practicing. / I suggest that you practice.

words.simpleMeaning: to give all your attention to something

sentences.daily: Concentrate on your studies.
sentences.office: She concentrates fully during meetings.
sentences.interview: I concentrate on what matters most.
sentences.business: Concentrate your resources on high-value tasks.
sentences.formal: He concentrates on quality over quantity.

words.usageNote: 'Concentrate on' = focus on. Imperative use: Concentrate! Stay focused!

words.simpleMeaning: to direct attention to one thing

sentences.daily: Focus on today's lesson.
sentences.office: She focuses on results, not just effort.
sentences.interview: I focus on learning and growing continuously.
sentences.business: Focus on solving the customer's problem.
sentences.formal: He focuses on strategic priorities.

words.usageNote: 'Focus on' or 'focus at' (less common). Imperative: Focus! Stay focused!

words.simpleMeaning: to watch something carefully and notice details

sentences.daily: Observe how native speakers talk.
sentences.office: She observes market trends daily.
sentences.interview: I observe and learn from senior colleagues.
sentences.business: Observe customer behaviour carefully.
sentences.formal: He observes all protocols strictly.
sentences.informal: Just observe and don't speak yet.

words.usageNote: More formal than 'watch'. 'Observe' implies careful, deliberate attention.

words.simpleMeaning: to confirm that something is true or correct

sentences.daily: Verify your answers before submitting.
sentences.office: She verifies all data before the report.
sentences.interview: I always verify facts before presenting.
sentences.business: Please verify the client's details.
sentences.formal: He verifies all claims with evidence.

words.usageNote: Imperative use: Verify the answer! 'Verify' is more formal than 'check'.

words.simpleMeaning: to officially allow something

sentences.daily: Please permit me to speak.
sentences.office: She permits flexible working hours.
sentences.interview: I am permitted to share this information.
sentences.business: Permits are needed for construction.
sentences.formal: He permits exceptions in special cases.

words.usageNote: More formal than 'allow'. 'Permit' as noun: a work permit.

words.simpleMeaning: to make yourself or something ready

sentences.daily: Prepare your notes every night.
sentences.office: She prepares agendas for all meetings.
sentences.interview: Always prepare thoroughly for interviews.
sentences.business: Prepare a detailed business proposal.
sentences.formal: He prepares comprehensive reports monthly.

words.usageNote: Imperative: Prepare! 'Be prepared' = Boy Scout motto. Very common in professional contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to organize activities to work together

sentences.daily: Coordinate your study schedule.
sentences.office: She coordinates all project activities.
sentences.interview: I coordinated a team of 10 for a product launch.
sentences.business: Coordinate with all stakeholders before proceeding.
sentences.formal: He coordinates cross-departmental efforts.

words.usageNote: 'Coordinate with' = work together with. Professional context: coordinate resources, timelines.

words.simpleMeaning: to arrange things in an orderly way

sentences.daily: Organize your study materials.
sentences.office: She organizes the monthly review meetings.
sentences.interview: I am well-organized and detail-oriented.
sentences.business: Organize your team around clear goals.
sentences.formal: He organizes resources efficiently.

words.usageNote: Imperative: Organize your work! 'Organized' as adjective: an organized person.

words.simpleMeaning: to hand in a document or proposal

sentences.daily: Submit your homework on time.
sentences.office: She submits reports every Friday.
sentences.interview: I submitted my resume last week.
sentences.business: Please submit the invoice by month end.
sentences.formal: He submitted a detailed proposal.

words.usageNote: Imperative: Submit now! 'Submission' is the noun. 'Submit to' someone = hand in to someone.

words.simpleMeaning: to go to an event or be present

sentences.daily: Attend every class regularly.
sentences.office: She attends all board meetings.
sentences.interview: I attended a leadership workshop last year.
sentences.business: Please attend the client presentation.
sentences.formal: He attends every conference in his field.

words.usageNote: 'Attend' (formal) vs 'come to' (informal). 'Attendance' is the noun.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling or showing joy

sentences.daily: I am happy today.
sentences.office: She is happy with her new role.
sentences.interview: I am happy to learn new skills.
sentences.business: Happy customers are loyal customers.
sentences.formal: He is happy to assist at any time.

words.usageNote: Be verb sentence: I am happy. He is happy. They are happy. Day 4 core word.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling the need for rest or sleep

sentences.daily: I am tired after work.
sentences.office: She is tired but keeps working.
sentences.interview: I never let myself get too tired.
sentences.business: Tired teams produce poor results.
sentences.formal: He was tired after the long conference.

words.usageNote: 'Tired' with be verb: I am tired / She was tired / They were tired. Day 4 topic.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling very happy and enthusiastic

sentences.daily: I am excited about today's lesson.
sentences.office: She is excited about the new project.
sentences.interview: I am very excited about this opportunity.
sentences.business: Investors are excited about the startup.
sentences.formal: He was excited to address the conference.

words.usageNote: 'Excited about' = enthusiastic about something. Be verb required: I am excited.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling sure about yourself or something

sentences.daily: I am confident in English now.
sentences.office: She is confident in her decisions.
sentences.interview: I am confident I can do this job.
sentences.business: Confident brands attract customers.
sentences.formal: He was confident during the presentation.

words.usageNote: 'Confident about/in': I am confident in my abilities. Day 4 be verb usage.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling anxious or worried

sentences.daily: I was nervous before the test.
sentences.office: She was nervous before the presentation.
sentences.interview: I am a little nervous but well-prepared.
sentences.business: Never appear nervous in negotiations.
sentences.formal: He was nervous but composed.

words.usageNote: 'Nervous about': I am nervous about the interview. Used with be verb: am/is/are/was/were.

words.simpleMeaning: having a lot to do; occupied

sentences.daily: I am busy with my studies.
sentences.office: She is busy in a meeting right now.
sentences.interview: I am always busy learning something new.
sentences.business: Busy people are productive people.
sentences.formal: He is busy with the annual report.

words.usageNote: 'Be busy with': I am busy with work. 'Busy' as adverb: busily working.

words.simpleMeaning: free and able to be used or met with

sentences.daily: I am available every evening.
sentences.office: She is available for a call at 3 PM.
sentences.interview: I am available to join immediately.
sentences.business: Our support is available 24/7.
sentences.formal: He will be available after the meeting.

words.usageNote: 'Available for': I am available for meetings. 'Available to': available to all customers.

words.simpleMeaning: prepared and in a fit state for something

sentences.daily: I am ready for the test.
sentences.office: She is ready with the presentation.
sentences.interview: I am ready to take on new challenges.
sentences.business: The product is ready for launch.
sentences.formal: He was ready before everyone else.
sentences.informal: Ready or not, here I come!

words.usageNote: 'Ready for': I am ready for work. 'Ready to': I am ready to start. Both are used.

words.simpleMeaning: not present in a place

sentences.daily: I was absent from school yesterday.
sentences.office: She was absent on Monday.
sentences.interview: I have never been absent without reason.
sentences.business: When the leader is absent, work suffers.
sentences.formal: He was absent from the conference.

words.usageNote: 'Absent from' a place. 'Absent-minded' = forgetful. Opposite: present.

words.simpleMeaning: in good physical condition

sentences.daily: I am healthy and active.
sentences.office: She is healthy because she exercises daily.
sentences.interview: I am healthy and can handle long hours.
sentences.business: A healthy workforce is productive.
sentences.formal: He was healthy and energetic throughout.

words.usageNote: Be verb + healthy: I am healthy. He was healthy. They are healthy.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling pleased about something you have achieved

sentences.daily: I am proud of my progress.
sentences.office: She is proud of her team's work.
sentences.interview: I am proud of completing the 75-day challenge.
sentences.business: We are proud to serve our customers.
sentences.formal: He was proud to represent the organization.

words.usageNote: 'Proud of': I am proud of you. Day 4 be verb usage.

words.simpleMeaning: having great physical or mental power

sentences.daily: I am strong because I work hard.
sentences.office: She has a strong work ethic.
sentences.interview: I have a strong background in finance.
sentences.business: Build a strong brand identity.
sentences.formal: He was strong in both analysis and communication.

words.usageNote: 'Strong' with be verb: I am strong. 'Strong in': strong in mathematics, strong in communication.

words.simpleMeaning: having great value or significance

sentences.daily: English is important for my career.
sentences.office: This meeting is very important.
sentences.interview: Time management is important to me.
sentences.business: Customer feedback is important for growth.
sentences.formal: He was important in shaping the strategy.

words.usageNote: Very common with be verb: is/are/was/were important.

words.simpleMeaning: not easy; requiring effort or skill

sentences.daily: This grammar is difficult.
sentences.office: The project was difficult but we succeeded.
sentences.interview: Difficult situations teach the most.
sentences.business: It is difficult to retain top talent.
sentences.formal: He found the task difficult initially.

words.usageNote: 'Difficult to': It is difficult to understand. 'Difficult for': It was difficult for me.

words.simpleMeaning: able to help or be used for something

sentences.daily: This lesson is very useful.
sentences.office: She is useful in client meetings.
sentences.interview: My skills are useful for this role.
sentences.business: This tool is useful for data analysis.
sentences.formal: He was useful in bridging both teams.

words.usageNote: 'Useful for': useful for learning. 'Useful to': useful to the team.

words.simpleMeaning: used to identify a specific thing near you

sentences.daily: This is my book.
sentences.office: This is the project report.
sentences.interview: This is my strongest skill.
sentences.business: This is our best product.
sentences.formal: This document requires your signature.

words.usageNote: 'This' = singular, nearby. Day 5 demonstrative. This + is + noun (singular).

words.simpleMeaning: used to identify a specific thing further away

sentences.daily: That is my house.
sentences.office: That was an excellent presentation.
sentences.interview: That experience changed my career.
sentences.business: That strategy worked very well.
sentences.formal: That proposal requires further review.

words.usageNote: 'That' = singular, farther away. Day 5: That + is + noun. 'That' also used as conjunction.

words.simpleMeaning: used to identify specific things near you (plural)

sentences.daily: These are my books.
sentences.office: These are the project files.
sentences.interview: These are my key achievements.
sentences.business: These are our top products.
sentences.formal: These documents need signatures.

words.usageNote: 'These' = plural, nearby. Day 5: These + are + plural noun.

words.simpleMeaning: used to identify specific things further away (plural)

sentences.daily: Those are my friends.
sentences.office: Those were the best results we got.
sentences.interview: Those challenges shaped who I am.
sentences.business: Those customers are our priority.
sentences.formal: Those proposals were submitted last week.

words.usageNote: 'Those' = plural, farther away. Day 5: Those + are + plural noun.

words.simpleMeaning: not far away; close in distance

sentences.daily: There is a school nearby.
sentences.office: Our office is nearby the metro station.
sentences.interview: I live nearby and can start immediately.
sentences.business: The nearby market is our target area.
sentences.formal: A hospital is situated nearby.

words.usageNote: Adverb/adjective. 'Nearby' = close in distance. Related to demonstratives (this/these = nearby).

words.simpleMeaning: far away in space or time

sentences.daily: That is a distant goal for me.
sentences.office: Distant clients need extra attention.
sentences.interview: That was a distant memory now.
sentences.business: Distant markets are new opportunities.
sentences.formal: He appears distant in large meetings.
sentences.informal: Why are you so distant today?

words.usageNote: Related to 'that/those' (farther). Also means emotionally withdrawn: He seemed distant.

words.simpleMeaning: clearly defined or identified

sentences.daily: This specific word is very useful.
sentences.office: She gives specific feedback.
sentences.interview: Give specific examples in interviews.
sentences.business: Target specific customer segments.
sentences.formal: He mentioned specific areas for improvement.

words.usageNote: 'This specific' / 'That specific' — demonstratives used with 'specific'. 'Specifically' is the adverb.

words.simpleMeaning: used to say something is distinct or special

sentences.daily: This particular word is new to me.
sentences.office: That particular project was challenging.
sentences.interview: In this particular role, I can contribute greatly.
sentences.business: That particular client is very important.
sentences.formal: He paid attention to this particular detail.

words.usageNote: 'This/that particular' strengthens demonstratives. 'In particular' = especially.

words.simpleMeaning: able to be seen

sentences.daily: This stain is very visible.
sentences.office: She keeps visible progress charts.
sentences.interview: My achievements are visible in my results.
sentences.business: Brand visibility is key in marketing.
sentences.formal: These problems are clearly visible in the data.

words.usageNote: 'Visible to': visible to everyone. This/these + is/are + visible — Day 5 connection.

words.simpleMeaning: easily noticed or understood; clear

sentences.daily: This mistake is obvious.
sentences.office: That was an obvious error.
sentences.interview: It's obvious I am passionate about this.
sentences.business: The solution is obvious once you see the data.
sentences.formal: These improvements are obvious in the results.

words.usageNote: 'Obviously' is the adverb. Common with 'this is obvious' — Day 5 be verb + demonstrative.

words.simpleMeaning: next to or adjoining something

sentences.daily: This room is adjacent to mine.
sentences.office: The adjacent department handles accounts.
sentences.interview: I worked in an adjacent role before.
sentences.business: The adjacent market segment is growing.
sentences.formal: Those buildings are adjacent to our campus.

words.usageNote: Formal word. 'Adjacent to' = next to. Used in spatial/physical descriptions.

words.simpleMeaning: relating to the centre; most important

sentences.daily: This is central to my learning plan.
sentences.office: That is the central issue in this project.
sentences.interview: Communication is central to my role.
sentences.business: Customer satisfaction is central to our strategy.
sentences.formal: These values are central to the organization.
sentences.informal: It's central to everything we do.

words.usageNote: 'Central to' = most important for. Connects with demonstratives: this/that is central.

words.simpleMeaning: clearly seen or understood

sentences.daily: This improvement is evident in my scores.
sentences.office: That problem was evident from day one.
sentences.interview: It is evident that I am passionate about this.
sentences.business: The growth is evident in these numbers.
sentences.formal: These results make the trend evident.

words.usageNote: 'It is evident that...' is a formal structure. Day 5 + Day 4 combined: demonstrative + be verb.

words.simpleMeaning: completely different; on the other side

sentences.daily: That building is opposite to mine.
sentences.office: She sits opposite me in the office.
sentences.interview: My approach is the opposite of micromanagement.
sentences.business: The opposite market segment needs different strategies.
sentences.formal: Those results are the opposite of what was expected.

words.usageNote: 'Opposite to' or 'opposite of'. Also used as adjective: the opposite side.

words.simpleMeaning: towards the front; in the direction ahead

sentences.daily: I look forward to learning every day.
sentences.office: Move the project forward this week.
sentences.interview: I look forward to contributing to your team.
sentences.business: We are moving forward with the plan.
sentences.formal: These steps take the company forward.

words.usageNote: 'Look forward to + ing' = excited about something coming. Very common professional phrase.

words.simpleMeaning: to have or own something

sentences.daily: I possess good communication skills.
sentences.office: She possesses the required qualifications.
sentences.interview: I possess all the skills needed for this job.
sentences.business: This company possesses strong market position.
sentences.formal: He possesses exceptional analytical ability.
sentences.informal: Do you possess a driving licence?

words.usageNote: 'Possess' = formal way to say 'have'. Stative verb — don't use in continuous form.

words.simpleMeaning: to have something as your possession

sentences.daily: I own a bicycle.
sentences.office: She owns her work completely.
sentences.interview: I have owned projects end-to-end.
sentences.business: They own the intellectual property.
sentences.formal: He owns extensive property abroad.

words.usageNote: 'Own' as verb = possess. 'Own up' = admit to a mistake. Stative verb.

words.simpleMeaning: to keep something in good condition or continue it

sentences.daily: I maintain a daily study schedule.
sentences.office: She maintains accurate records.
sentences.interview: I have maintained high performance standards.
sentences.business: Maintain good client relationships.
sentences.formal: He maintains excellent work quality.

words.usageNote: 'Maintain' has more of a formal register. Day 6: He has maintained → has + V3.

words.simpleMeaning: to gain or come to have something

sentences.daily: I have acquired new vocabulary today.
sentences.office: She has acquired excellent project skills.
sentences.interview: I have acquired expertise in digital tools.
sentences.business: We have acquired two new clients.
sentences.formal: He has acquired significant industry experience.
sentences.informal: Have you acquired the ticket yet?

words.usageNote: 'Acquire' is formal. Day 6: I have acquired (Present Perfect). Skills, knowledge, assets are 'acquired'.

words.simpleMeaning: the right or ability to use something

sentences.daily: I have access to the internet.
sentences.office: She has access to all client files.
sentences.interview: I have accessed advanced training platforms.
sentences.business: Do you have access to the database?
sentences.formal: He has access to restricted information.

words.usageNote: 'Have access to' = be able to use. Day 6 have/has usage.

words.simpleMeaning: to show or prove something clearly

sentences.daily: I demonstrate my skills every day.
sentences.office: She demonstrates excellent leadership.
sentences.interview: I have demonstrated strong performance.
sentences.business: These numbers demonstrate our growth.
sentences.formal: He has demonstrated consistent excellence.

words.usageNote: Day 6: She has demonstrated (Present Perfect). Common interview word.

words.simpleMeaning: to successfully do or finish something

sentences.daily: I have accomplished my daily goals.
sentences.office: She has accomplished all her targets.
sentences.interview: I have accomplished a 40% growth in sales.
sentences.business: What have you accomplished this quarter?
sentences.formal: He has accomplished remarkable results.

words.usageNote: 'Accomplish' sounds more formal. Day 6: I have accomplished = Present Perfect. Great for interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: to carry out or satisfy a requirement or promise

sentences.daily: I have fulfilled my promise.
sentences.office: She has fulfilled all the criteria.
sentences.interview: I have fulfilled every target in my career.
sentences.business: We have fulfilled 100% of orders this month.
sentences.formal: He has fulfilled the requirements of the role.

words.usageNote: 'Fulfill a promise/target/requirement' — very professional. Day 6: has/have + fulfilled.

words.simpleMeaning: to give things out to a number of people

sentences.daily: I have distributed the notes to everyone.
sentences.office: She distributes work evenly in the team.
sentences.interview: I have distributed tasks across team members.
sentences.business: We distribute products across 10 states.
sentences.formal: He has distributed the report to all stakeholders.
sentences.informal: Have you distributed the forms yet?

words.usageNote: 'Distribute to' = give to. Day 6: She has distributed (Present Perfect).

words.simpleMeaning: to carry out or put a plan into action

sentences.daily: I execute my study plan every day.
sentences.office: She executes strategies flawlessly.
sentences.interview: I have executed large-scale projects.
sentences.business: We have executed over 50 campaigns.
sentences.formal: He has executed the plan within budget.

words.usageNote: Day 6 + 7 usage: I have executed (Day 6), I had executed (Day 7). Formal business word.

words.simpleMeaning: to make practical use of something

sentences.daily: I have utilized my time well today.
sentences.office: She has utilized all available resources.
sentences.interview: I have utilized data to drive decisions.
sentences.business: Have you utilized the new software?
sentences.formal: He has utilized industry best practices.

words.usageNote: 'Utilize' is more formal than 'use'. Day 6: I have utilized. Often used in reports and interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: to provide something that is needed

sentences.daily: I have supplied all necessary materials.
sentences.office: She has supplied data for the presentation.
sentences.interview: I have supplied solutions to complex problems.
sentences.business: We have supplied 1000 units this month.
sentences.formal: He has supplied all required documentation.

words.usageNote: 'Supply to/with': supply materials to clients. Day 6: has/have + supplied.

words.simpleMeaning: to create or produce something

sentences.daily: I have generated ideas for improvement.
sentences.office: She has generated consistent revenue.
sentences.interview: I have generated new leads for the team.
sentences.business: We have generated 20% growth this year.
sentences.formal: He has generated valuable insights.
sentences.informal: Have you generated any ideas yet?

words.usageNote: 'Generate revenue/ideas/leads' — common business usage. Day 6 Present Perfect.

words.simpleMeaning: to bring a memory back to mind

sentences.daily: I recall learning this word last week.
sentences.office: She recalled all the details of the meeting.
sentences.interview: I recall overcoming a major challenge.
sentences.business: Can you recall the exact figures?
sentences.formal: He recalled the client's specific requirements.

words.usageNote: Day 7 word — past recall. 'Recall' is more formal than 'remember'.

words.simpleMeaning: to meet or face unexpectedly

sentences.daily: I had encountered this problem before.
sentences.office: She had encountered the issue earlier.
sentences.interview: I had encountered difficult clients and resolved issues.
sentences.business: We had encountered delays in the supply chain.
sentences.formal: He had encountered resistance from the team.

words.usageNote: Day 7: I had encountered (Past Perfect). Formal word for 'come across'.

words.simpleMeaning: to suffer through something difficult

sentences.daily: I had endured many failures before succeeding.
sentences.office: She had endured a difficult period at work.
sentences.interview: I had endured challenges and emerged stronger.
sentences.business: The company had endured the recession well.
sentences.formal: He had endured considerable pressure.

words.usageNote: Day 7: had + endured (Past Perfect). Used to show something difficult in the past before another event.

words.simpleMeaning: to continue to live or exist despite difficulty

sentences.daily: I had survived many tests before this one.
sentences.office: The team had survived major restructuring.
sentences.interview: I had survived my first job's tough environment.
sentences.business: The company had survived the economic downturn.
sentences.formal: He had survived the most challenging phase.

words.usageNote: Day 7: had survived. 'Survive' used literally and figuratively.

words.simpleMeaning: to find a solution to a problem

sentences.daily: I had resolved this issue before.
sentences.office: She had resolved the conflict before the meeting.
sentences.interview: I had resolved a team dispute effectively.
sentences.business: We had resolved all client complaints.
sentences.formal: He had resolved the matter diplomatically.

words.usageNote: Day 7: had resolved (Past Perfect). 'Resolve to do' = decide firmly: I resolved to improve.

words.simpleMeaning: to give something toward a shared goal

sentences.daily: I had contributed to every team project.
sentences.office: She had contributed innovative ideas.
sentences.interview: I had contributed to a 25% sales increase.
sentences.business: Each team member had contributed to the success.
sentences.formal: He had contributed significantly to the research.

words.usageNote: Day 7: had contributed. 'Contribute to' = add to. Never 'contribute in'.

words.simpleMeaning: to make or become better

sentences.daily: I had improved my score by 30%.
sentences.office: She had improved the process significantly.
sentences.interview: I had improved team output by 20%.
sentences.business: The product had improved over time.
sentences.formal: He had improved the system's efficiency.

words.usageNote: Day 7: had improved (Past Perfect). Shows improvement before a past point. 'Improve on' = do better than before.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something more modern or add new information

sentences.daily: I had updated my notes before class.
sentences.office: She had updated the report before the meeting.
sentences.interview: I had updated my resume multiple times.
sentences.business: We had updated our pricing before the launch.
sentences.formal: He had updated all stakeholders.

words.usageNote: Day 7: had updated (Past Perfect). 'Update someone on something' = inform them of latest news.

words.simpleMeaning: to examine or assess something again

sentences.daily: I had reviewed all my notes.
sentences.office: She had reviewed the proposal before sharing.
sentences.interview: I had reviewed the company's annual report.
sentences.business: We had reviewed the contract carefully.
sentences.formal: He had reviewed every clause of the agreement.

words.usageNote: Day 7: had reviewed. 'Review' is both noun and verb. 'Under review' = being examined.

words.simpleMeaning: to show or introduce something to an audience

sentences.daily: I had presented my project before the break.
sentences.office: She had presented to the board last quarter.
sentences.interview: I had presented a strategy that doubled revenue.
sentences.business: We had presented the proposal before the deadline.
sentences.formal: He had presented his findings to senior management.

words.usageNote: Day 7: had presented. Both verb and adjective (present = here/now). 'Present' stress: verb = /prɪˈzent/, noun/adj = /ˈprezənt/.

words.simpleMeaning: to decide in advance what you will do

sentences.daily: By next year, I will have planned my career path.
sentences.office: By December, she will have planned the annual event.
sentences.interview: By the end of this year, I will have planned my MBA.
sentences.business: By Q4, we will have planned the expansion.
sentences.formal: He will have planned the entire campaign by then.
sentences.informal: Will you have planned it by then?

words.usageNote: Day 8: will have planned. Future Perfect — action will be complete before a future time.

words.simpleMeaning: a goal or objective to work toward

sentences.daily: By next month, I will have hit my target.
sentences.office: By Friday, she will have reached her target.
sentences.interview: By year end, I will have exceeded my targets.
sentences.business: By Q3, we will have targeted 1000 new customers.
sentences.formal: He will have achieved his targets before the deadline.
sentences.informal: Will you have hit the target?

words.usageNote: Day 8: will have targeted/reached target. Future Perfect. 'Target' as noun: hit/meet/exceed target.

words.simpleMeaning: the latest time by which something must be done

sentences.daily: By tomorrow, I will have met the deadline.
sentences.office: She will have finished before the deadline.
sentences.interview: I never miss a deadline.
sentences.business: By Monday, we will have submitted before the deadline.
sentences.formal: He will have delivered well ahead of the deadline.

words.usageNote: 'Meet/miss the deadline' — very common professional phrases. Day 8 Future Perfect context.

words.simpleMeaning: an important stage in a project or process

sentences.daily: By Day 75, I will have crossed every milestone.
sentences.office: By December, the team will have hit all milestones.
sentences.interview: I will have achieved key milestones before joining.
sentences.business: By launch day, we will have cleared all milestones.
sentences.formal: He will have completed the project milestones.
sentences.informal: Will you have reached that milestone?

words.usageNote: Day 8 Future Perfect. 'Hit/reach/achieve a milestone' — business language.

words.simpleMeaning: to plan something for a specific time

sentences.daily: By morning, I will have scheduled my study plan.
sentences.office: She will have scheduled all meetings by 9 AM.
sentences.interview: I will have scheduled a demo by next week.
sentences.business: By Friday, we will have scheduled the review.
sentences.formal: He will have scheduled the conference by then.
sentences.informal: Will you have scheduled it by then?

words.usageNote: Day 8: will have scheduled. 'On schedule' = on time. 'Behind schedule' = delayed.

words.simpleMeaning: to predict what will happen in the future

sentences.daily: By end of year, analysts will have forecast growth.
sentences.office: She will have forecast the sales by Q3.
sentences.interview: I will have forecast performance milestones.
sentences.business: By then, we will have forecast next year's budget.
sentences.formal: He will have forecast trends accurately.
sentences.informal: Will they have forecast this correctly?

words.usageNote: Day 8: will have forecast. Future Perfect. 'Weather forecast' = weather prediction.

words.simpleMeaning: to expect or predict something will happen

sentences.daily: By then, I will have anticipated all challenges.
sentences.office: By launch, she will have anticipated customer needs.
sentences.interview: I always anticipate questions in advance.
sentences.business: By next year, we will have anticipated market shifts.
sentences.formal: He will have anticipated the risks.

words.usageNote: Day 8: will have anticipated. 'Anticipate' sounds more formal and professional than 'expect'.

words.simpleMeaning: to pledge or dedicate yourself to something

sentences.daily: By next week, I will have committed to a daily routine.
sentences.office: She will have committed to the new strategy by then.
sentences.interview: I commit to delivering results in 90 days.
sentences.business: By signing, we will have committed to the partnership.
sentences.formal: He will have committed resources by next quarter.
sentences.informal: Will you have committed by then?

words.usageNote: Day 8: will have committed. 'Committed' as adjective: a committed employee.

words.simpleMeaning: to start or introduce something new officially

sentences.daily: By next month, I will have launched my new routine.
sentences.office: By March, she will have launched the product.
sentences.interview: I will have launched three products in my career.
sentences.business: By Q2, we will have launched in 5 new cities.
sentences.formal: He will have launched the initiative by year end.
sentences.informal: Will you have launched it by then?

words.usageNote: Day 8: will have launched. 'Product launch' = official introduction of a product.

words.simpleMeaning: to go beyond or exceed a goal or expectation

sentences.daily: By end of the challenge, I will have surpassed my goals.
sentences.office: By December, she will have surpassed all targets.
sentences.interview: I will have surpassed the KPIs set for this role.
sentences.business: By year end, we will have surpassed last year's revenue.
sentences.formal: He will have surpassed all performance benchmarks.
sentences.informal: Will you have surpassed your target?

words.usageNote: Day 8: will have surpassed. Strong word for exceeding expectations. Great for interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: to increase speed or make something happen faster

sentences.daily: By next month, I will have accelerated my learning.
sentences.office: By June, she will have accelerated production.
sentences.interview: I accelerate results by planning ahead.
sentences.business: By Q4, we will have accelerated market entry.
sentences.formal: He will have accelerated the timeline.
sentences.informal: Will you have accelerated by then?

words.usageNote: Day 8: will have accelerated. 'Accelerate' is formal. Used in business for growth and timelines.

words.simpleMeaning: to be real or present; to have being

sentences.daily: There exist many options for learning.
sentences.office: A problem exists in the current process.
sentences.interview: Various opportunities exist in this company.
sentences.business: There exists a market gap we can fill.
sentences.formal: These challenges exist across all departments.

words.usageNote: Day 9 connection: 'There exist' is formal form of 'There are'. 'Exist' is stative — avoid continuous.

words.simpleMeaning: to come into view; to seem to be

sentences.daily: There appears to be a mistake.
sentences.office: A new opportunity has appeared.
sentences.interview: There appears to be a match for my skills.
sentences.business: New competitors have appeared in the market.
sentences.formal: There appear to be several errors.

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'There appears to be' = formal alternative to 'There is'. 'Appear to be' is very professional.

words.simpleMeaning: to continue to exist; to stay

sentences.daily: There remains a lot to learn.
sentences.office: There remain several unresolved issues.
sentences.interview: There remain opportunities for me to grow.
sentences.business: There remains a gap in the market.
sentences.formal: There remain significant challenges.

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'There remains/remain' = formal way to say 'There is/are still'. 'Remain' is more formal than 'stay'.

words.simpleMeaning: to live in or occupy a place

sentences.daily: Many people inhabit this city.
sentences.office: Many employees inhabit this floor.
sentences.interview: Various professionals inhabit this field.
sentences.business: Many brands inhabit the same space.
sentences.formal: There are many species that inhabit this area.

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'There are many who inhabit'. Formal word for 'live in'. Related to There is/are.

words.simpleMeaning: to be present in; to fill a space

sentences.daily: There are 10 students who occupy this classroom.
sentences.office: There are 5 teams that occupy this floor.
sentences.interview: I currently occupy a team lead role.
sentences.business: Our brand occupies the premium segment.
sentences.formal: These offices are occupied by the legal team.

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'This seat is occupied' connects with There is/are (There is a person in this seat).

words.simpleMeaning: to have something inside

sentences.daily: There is a box that contains my notes.
sentences.office: The folder contains all project files.
sentences.interview: My portfolio contains my best work.
sentences.business: The package contains all necessary documents.
sentences.formal: There are files that contain confidential data.

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'There is a box that contains...' Links container with There is/are.

words.simpleMeaning: to provide space or make room for someone

sentences.daily: There is a hostel that accommodates students.
sentences.office: The meeting room accommodates 20 people.
sentences.interview: I accommodate different work styles easily.
sentences.business: There are halls that accommodate 500 guests.
sentences.formal: The facility accommodates all accessibility needs.
sentences.informal: Can this room accommodate us all?

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'There is a room that accommodates...' — There is/are + noun + accommodates.

words.simpleMeaning: to show or point to something

sentences.daily: There is a sign that indicates the exit.
sentences.office: The data indicates a problem.
sentences.interview: There are indicators that show my suitability.
sentences.business: There are signs that indicate market recovery.
sentences.formal: There are metrics that indicate strong performance.

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'There is/are + noun + that indicate(s)'. 'Indicate' is formal; 'show' is informal.

words.simpleMeaning: to find the position of something

sentences.daily: There is an app that locates buses.
sentences.office: There is a tool that locates all files.
sentences.interview: I can locate solutions quickly.
sentences.business: There are systems to locate delivery packages.
sentences.formal: The office is located in the city centre.
sentences.informal: Can you locate this for me?

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'There is a + noun + that locates'. 'Located' as past participle: The office is located in Delhi.

words.simpleMeaning: to discover or identify something

sentences.daily: There is software that detects errors.
sentences.office: There are systems that detect security threats.
sentences.interview: I can detect problems before they escalate.
sentences.business: There are tools that detect market trends.
sentences.formal: There are sensors that detect temperature changes.

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'There is a tool that detects...' — Connects There is/are with function clauses.

words.simpleMeaning: to see an event happen; to observe

sentences.daily: There were many who witnessed the accident.
sentences.office: There was no one who witnessed the agreement.
sentences.interview: I have witnessed huge organizational transformation.
sentences.business: There are clients who have witnessed our growth.
sentences.formal: There were several witnesses at the scene.

words.usageNote: Day 9: 'There were many witnesses' — plural noun with were. Both noun and verb.

words.simpleMeaning: the total number of people in a place

sentences.daily: There is a large population in this city.
sentences.office: There is a growing population of remote workers.
sentences.interview: There is a diverse population in my city.
